nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; benthic species inhabiting rubble and algal-covered patches of dead reef in 1–6 m depth

diet.notes: The Queensland Dwarf Goby grazes on detritus and microcrustaceans such as copepods and amphipods in its natural habitat. It has been documented accepting prepared aquarium foods as well as live offerings, though maintaining a healthy population of live copepods is generally advised to supplement its diet.

summary: The Queensland Dwarf Goby is one of the smallest reef gobies available in the aquarium hobby, reaching around 2.4 cm as an adult. Generally considered peaceful and reef-safe, it tends to form small shoals and is well suited to nano and pico reef setups.
careNotes:
The Queensland Dwarf Goby (Eviota queenslandica) is one of the smallest gobies available in the aquarium hobby, typically reaching around 2.4 cm as an adult. It is generally considered reef-safe and peaceful toward corals and most invertebrates. Captive-bred specimens — such as those produced through dedicated marine breeding programs — tend to acclimate to aquarium life more readily than wild-caught individuals.

This species has been observed forming small shoals in the wild among coral rubble, and captive groups are reported to coexist peacefully, which is a notable trait among gobies. It is well suited to nano and pico reef setups as well as established reef aquariums. A healthy population of copepods and amphipods is beneficial; a refugium (a small, separate chamber connected to the display tank and seeded with live microfauna) can help sustain this prey base over time.

Due to its very small size, care should be taken to protect against strong filter intakes — sponge pre-filters over inlet tubes are recommended. A tightly-fitted lid or mesh cover is also advisable, as gobies in this genus tend to jump.

The lifespan of Eviota gobies is notably short by aquarium standards. Published studies on the genus document maximum wild lifespans of roughly 59–90 days, with a generation time of approximately 0.2 years (around 73 days). Captive specimens may live somewhat longer under stable conditions, but keepers should expect a short-lived fish and not interpret natural senescence as a sign of disease.
breedingNotes: Spawning in Eviota species has been documented to follow semilunar or 14-day cycles. Captive breeding information for Eviota queenslandica specifically is not well-documented in available hobby sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Semilunar (approximately 14-day) spawning cycles have been documented in wild Eviota populations. Captive breeding of this species specifically is not well-documented in available hobby sources.
